patterns.name: Open with Recognition, Not Brand
patterns.description: Delay brand reveal. Open with a moment the viewer recognizes — relatable problem, visual surprise, or curiosity gap. Never a logo.
patterns.exception_note: Early brand works ONLY when brand identity is essential to the creative surprise (inversion, reverse psychology).
patterns.mechanism: Logo-first triggers ad-skip instinct. A human moment activates curiosity before the rational ad-filter engages.

patterns.name: Curiosity Engine — Open Loops and Escalation
patterns.description: Create an unanswered question or escalating absurdity that the viewer must resolve by watching.
patterns.mechanism: Zeigarnik Effect — the brain cannot tolerate open loops. Unanswered questions create cognitive tension that only resolves by watching.

patterns.name: 30 Seconds is the Sweet Spot
patterns.description: 30-second ads dominate for digital. 60s+ requires exceptional writing, talent, or retention mechanics.
patterns.exception_note: 60s works with A-list talent (Piyush Mishra), deep emotional narrative (Cadbury), mystery structure (Adani), or debate format (Tata Tea).
patterns.mechanism: 30s is long enough for problem-solution-CTA but short enough for most viewers to reach the end.

patterns.name: Pattern Interrupt at the Midpoint
patterns.description: Place a tonal shift, surprise, or energy change at 40-60% of the ad's duration to prevent drop-off.
patterns.mechanism: Attention naturally decays. A pattern interrupt resets the attention clock by signaling something new is happening.

patterns.name: Earned CTA — Story Logic Leads to Action
patterns.description: The CTA feels like the natural conclusion of the narrative, not an appended ask.
patterns.mechanism: A CTA that follows a compelling story feels like the viewer's own idea, not the brand's demand.

patterns.name: Sound-Off Survivability
patterns.description: Ads designed to communicate core message visually without audio, via visual comedy or bold text overlays.
patterns.exception_note: Dialogue-driven ads (Zomato, Cadbury, Tata Tea) do not work sound-off. Acceptable for YouTube but risky for Meta.
patterns.mechanism: 85% of Meta feed videos watched without sound. Audio-dependent ads lose majority of feed impressions.

patterns.name: Commodity-to-Emotion Reframe
patterns.description: Transform a commodity product into an emotional choice by attaching meaning beyond functional specs.
patterns.mechanism: When products are functionally interchangeable, purchase decisions shift to emotional territory: trust, identity, feeling.

patterns.name: Family as Protagonist
patterns.description: Place the family unit (parents + kids, 4-5 members) at the center for household products.
patterns.mechanism: Broadband is a household purchase. The decision-maker (parent) sees their family reflected. Kids create emotional stakes.

patterns.name: Celebrity as Voice, Not Face
patterns.description: Celebrity endorsements work when the celebrity embodies the brand voice, not just lends their face.
patterns.mechanism: Generic celebrity endorsement triggers ad-skepticism. Persona-aligned celebrity creates authentic communication.

anti_patterns.name: Brand-First Opening
anti_patterns.description: Opening with logo animation, brand jingle, or brand name before any story/hook.
anti_patterns.evidence: 0 of 13 best-in-class ads open with standalone logo animation.

anti_patterns.name: Speed-Test Advertising
anti_patterns.description: ISP ads that lead with speed numbers, tech specs, or buffer-wheel imagery.
anti_patterns.evidence: The only ISP ad in the library (Airtel Xstream) never mentions speed once.

anti_patterns.name: Flat Energy / No Pattern Interrupt
anti_patterns.description: Ads that maintain the same tone, pace, and energy throughout without any shift.
anti_patterns.evidence: All 13 ads have documented pace changes and pattern interrupts.

anti_patterns.name: Generic CTA
anti_patterns.description: CTAs that could be appended to any ad from any brand. Learn More. Visit Website.
anti_patterns.evidence: Strongest CTAs are narrative-connected. None of the top ads use generic CTAs.

anti_patterns.name: Cultural Tone-Deafness
anti_patterns.description: Missing the cultural register of the target audience. Too urban, too English, too preachy.
anti_patterns.evidence: Strongest ads precisely match cultural register: Meesho (Tier 2-3 Hindi), Tata Tea (family debate).

scores.5: Relatable moment, visual surprise, or curiosity gap. No brand-first. Matches C1+C2.
scores.4: Strong hook, minor issue (slightly slow or early brand without creative justification).
scores.3: Decent but no top pattern match. Functional not compelling.
scores.2: Weak hook. Partially matches anti-pattern X1 or X2.
scores.1: Logo animation opener. Speed claims first. Matches X1 or X2.

scores.5: Uses curiosity engine C2. Pattern interrupt at midpoint C4. No flat zone.
scores.4: Strong retention with clear pace changes. Minor flat zone.
scores.3: Watchable but no compelling reason to stay past 50%.
scores.2: Flat energy. Predictable. Likely drops at 15s.
scores.1: No retention mechanic. Same energy throughout. Matches X3.

scores.5: Story leads to action K1. Sound-off works K2. CTA narrative-connected.
scores.4: Strong but one gap (sound-off or CTA slightly disconnected).
scores.3: Functional. Product clear, CTA exists, no narrative drive.
scores.2: Product unclear until end. Generic CTA. No sound-off design.
scores.1: No reason to click. CTA afterthought. Matches X4.

scores.5: Specific CTA tied to objective. Earned by narrative. Urgency present. Low friction.
scores.4: Good CTA, minor issue (late timing or weak urgency).
scores.3: Generic but present. Learn more without specificity.
scores.2: CTA disconnected or buried. No urgency.

scores.5: Distinctive voice/aesthetic. Brand enhances creative. Intentional logo placement.
scores.4: Consistent voice. Appropriate placement. Minor inconsistency.
scores.3: Brand present but generic. Could be any competitor with logo swap.
scores.2: Brand feels forced or interrupts creative flow.
scores.1: No coherence. Logo slapped on. No distinctive voice.
